knownGaps: Article 17 grants forex accounts, but Foreign Exchange Code Article 6 still requires Central Bank authorization
Capital Market Decree (Décret 2018-1037) does not recognize SAFEs; Central Bank memo (Feb-2020) classifies them as debt
Commercial Code Article 154 enforces one-share-one-vote, blocking multi-class share structures
30% foreign-worker cap conflicts with talent strategy
notable: First African startup act; industry-led implementation model (Tunisian Startups association) outperformed pure government-led approaches.

recommendations.currentText: Mandates one-share-one-vote for sociétés anonymes
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Exempt Startup-Label-holding companies; permit multi-class share structures (voting, non-voting, preferred) with disclosure in the articles of association.

recommendations.currentText: Requires minimum seven shareholders for SA; SARL board of 3-12 directors
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Permit Startup-Label companies to incorporate as an SAS-equivalent form with minimum two shareholders and single-director governance.

recommendations.currentText: Requires Central Bank authorization for all forex accounts, contradicting Startup Act Article 17 which grants such accounts to labelled startups
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Article 6 to auto-recognize Startup Act Article 17 forex-account grants; no separate BCT authorization needed for labelled startups.

recommendations.currentText: Does not recognise SAFEs; BCT memo classifies SAFEs as debt for accounting and prudential purposes
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Décret 2018-1037 to recognise SAFEs and convertible notes as equity-linked securities when issued by Startup-Label companies. Withdraw or update the Feb-2020 BCT memo accordingly.

recommendations.code: Impôt sur le Revenu des Personnes Physiques (IRPP)
recommendations.currentText: Taxes equity grants to employees as immediate wages on theoretical valuations, despite Startup Act Articles 8-9 allowing equity compensation
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend IRPP Article 38 to defer taxation of equity grants in Startup-Label companies until a liquidity event (sale, IPO, or secondary).

recommendations.currentText: Caps foreign personnel at 30%, reverting to 10% after three years
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Raise the cap to 50% for Startup-Label companies, with local-talent-training commitments as a condition.

recommendations.currentText: No mutual recognition of Startup Label across AMU members despite shared regional framework
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral passport between Tunisia's Startup Label and Morocco's equivalent label once Morocco's framework activates. Tunisia-Algeria as a follow-on.

knownGaps: Tax benefits require separate DGID verification (typically 45-60 days vs 30-day statutory limit)
Article 12 mentions 'titres convertibles' but lacks OHADA integration for SAFEs/convertible notes
BCEAO Instruction 2012-09 only recognises traditional debt and equity
20% foreign-worker quota constrains hiring
No mutual recognition with neighbouring Ivory Coast despite identical ECOWAS membership
notable: DER/FJ acts as both labeling authority and seed funder — unique single-window model.

recommendations.currentText: Limits OHADA companies to SARL/SA/SAS forms with fixed capital, rigid governance, and default one-share-one-vote
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend AUSCGIE to introduce a Société par Actions Simplifiée-Innovation (SAS-I) — a modular vehicle with multi-class shares, flexible governance, and standardised convertible-instrument recognition. Requires OHADA Council of Ministers consensus across 17 member states.

recommendations.currentText: Startup Act mentions 'titres convertibles' but BCEAO Instruction 2012-09 only recognises traditional debt and equity
recommendations.proposedAmendment: BCEAO circular recognising SAFEs and convertible notes as equity-linked securities for Startup-Label companies in the UEMOA zone, with standardised prudential treatment.

recommendations.currentText: Impose rigid employment structures incompatible with equity-based compensation and project-based work
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Permit Startup-Label companies to grant equity compensation with tax deferred to liquidity, and to engage technical specialists under flexible project-based contracts.

recommendations.currentText: Tax benefits require separate DGID verification, typically 45-60 days against a 30-day statutory limit
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Tax benefits apply automatically from the date of Startup Label issuance. API integration between DER/FJ and DGID eliminates re-submission.

recommendations.currentText: Imposes 30-day repatriation requirements on export proceeds, incompatible with SaaS billing cycles
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Carve-out for digital-service exports by Startup-Label companies: quarterly rather than daily/30-day reporting, higher transaction limits, and recognition of stripe/paddle/wise-style payment processors.

recommendations.article: Senegal ↔ Ivory Coast (OHADA / UEMOA / ECOWAS pilot)
recommendations.currentText: No mutual recognition despite Senegal and Ivory Coast both being members of ECOWAS, OHADA, and UEMOA, with nearly identical Startup Act qualification criteria
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral passport: a startup labelled by DER/FJ is auto-recognised by Côte d'Ivoire's labelling committee within 14 days. Sector-first: fintech, then logistics, then e-commerce.

recommendations.currentText: Misaligns with the ECOWAS Supplementary Act on Personal Data Protection (2010)
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bring the national data-protection law into alignment with the ECOWAS Supplementary Act; recognise cross-border data flows to ECOWAS member states without additional adequacy decision for Startup-Label companies.

labelingBody: National Council for Digital Innovation & Entrepreneurship (NCDIE)
knownGaps: Schedule 3 tax incentives not integrated into federal tax system; FIRS coordination ongoing
SEC Rules 2013 classify SAFEs as debt instruments; many founders use Cayman/Delaware parents to issue SAFEs
CAMA Section 124 enforces one-share-one-vote, blocking multi-class share structures
Pension fund rules (PenCom) restrict institutional capital from flowing to startups
notable: Largest GDP among enacted-act countries; CBN regulatory sandbox is most mature on the continent.

recommendations.currentText: Explicitly prohibits dual-class voting for private companies
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Exempt NSA-labelled startups; permit multi-class share structures with disclosure in the memorandum and articles. Companies that lose the label revert to one-share-one-vote on a forward basis (no retroactive conversion).

recommendations.currentText: Limits recognised company types to private and public companies
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Recognise NSA-labelled startup as a class of private company with bespoke governance defaults aligned with Section 124 amendment.

recommendations.currentText: Classify SAFEs as debt instruments; Section 23 of NSA references convertibles but standard tools remain in a legal gray area
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend SEC Rules to recognise SAFEs and convertible notes as equity-linked securities when issued by NSA-labelled startups. Treat as equity for accounting, tax, and prudential reporting.

recommendations.code: Labor Act + Personal Income Tax Act
recommendations.currentText: No provisions for equity-based compensation; PIT taxes equity grants as immediate income at grant
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend the Labor Act and PIT to recognise ESOPs for NSA-labelled startups with taxation deferred to liquidity event. Standardised vesting and cliff provisions in the schedule.

recommendations.currentText: Requires CCIs for all foreign investments regardless of startup status; adds 4-6 weeks to routine cross-border payments
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Simplified forex framework for NSA-labelled startups: expedited CCI processing (under 5 business days), higher transaction limits, and recognition of standard fintech payment processors for non-resident customer billing.

recommendations.currentText: Pioneer-status tax incentives in NSA Schedule 3 not integrated into the federal tax system; FIRS coordination ongoing two years after enactment
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Finance Act amendment auto-recognising NSA label as pioneer status for FIRS purposes. API integration between the NSA portal and FIRS eliminates re-submission.

recommendations.code: National Office for Technology Acquisition and Promotion (NOTAP) Act
recommendations.currentText: NOTAP approval requirements add 2-6 months to technology-transfer arrangements, particularly for intra-group IP licensing
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Exempt intra-group IP licensing by NSA-labelled startups from NOTAP review. Maintain notification but not approval.

recommendations.code: PenCom Regulations on Investment of Pension Fund Assets
recommendations.currentText: Restricts pension funds from investing in startups, despite Africa-based investors now representing 35% of African startup deals
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend PenCom regulations to permit allocations to licensed startup funds (not direct startup equity) with prudential guardrails; target 2-3% of total AUM by 2028. Ghana's 5%-by-2026 mandate is the template.

recommendations.article: Treaty hooks: ECOWAS Article 59 (Free Movement); Protocol on Trade in Services (2019)
recommendations.currentText: Despite ECOWAS membership, no mutual recognition with neighbouring markets; expanding from Nigeria to Ghana or Senegal requires 60-90 days to establish operations
recommendations.proposedAmendment: ECOWAS-wide passport built on Article 59 + the Protocol on Trade in Services. Phase 1: NSA labelling auto-recognised by Ghana/Senegal/Ivory Coast for fintech, e-commerce, and logistics startups.

knownGaps: OHADA Uniform Act (2014) lacks provisions for complex cap tables and modern instruments
No legal recognition for SAFEs or convertible notes
Limited interface with banking and forex regulations
notable: Could harmonise immediately with Senegal and Ivory Coast under shared OHADA commercial code.

recommendations.currentText: Only SARL and SA structures available; fixed capital requirements and rigid governance procedures incompatible with startup volatility
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Joint OHADA SAS-I form (shared with Senegal, Ivory Coast) — modular vehicle with multi-class shares, flexible governance, and standardised convertible recognition.

recommendations.currentText: Immediate employment obligations incompatible with startup volatility; mobile-money ventures employ more agents than direct employees to avoid these restrictions
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Permit JEI-labelled startups to engage technical specialists under flexible project-based contracts; recognise ESOP grants with taxation deferred to liquidity.

recommendations.currentText: Requires complex declarations for cross-border capital movements; 45-60 day processing times consume 15-20% of operational budgets for JEI-labelled startups
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Expedited process for JEI-labelled startups: declarations completed in under 5 business days; simplified documentation for routine intra-group transactions.

recommendations.currentText: Despite 2022 EAC accession, no mechanism to recognise Rwanda or Kenya startup labelling and vice versa
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral pilots leveraging EAC Common Market Protocol Articles 13-14: JEI-labelled startups auto-recognised in Rwanda; Kigali's startup support extended to Kinshasa-based JEI holders.

recommendations.code: Loi sur les startups + Implementation decrees
recommendations.currentText: CNPE (National Council for Entrepreneurship Promotion) establishment pending; JEI digital portal in development; implementation decrees still being formulated three years after enactment
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Complete CNPE activation within 6 months. Deploy JEI digital portal with API integration to OHADA RCCM, DGI (tax), and the Central Bank.

recommendations.currentText: Graduated tax exemptions (100% → 50% over five years) defined but not yet operationally integrated with DGI
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Auto-apply graduated exemptions from the date of JEI label issuance; API connection between the labelling portal and DGI.

labelingBody: Digital Startup Labeling Committee (Ministry of Digital Transition)
knownGaps: No automatic recognition of Senegal's startup label despite shared ECOWAS/OHADA/UEMOA membership
OHADA constraints on multi-class shares carry over
SAFE/convertible-note recognition unresolved
notable: Most recent francophone West African enactment; strong candidate for OHADA-wide harmonisation pilot.

recommendations.currentText: OHADA Uniform Act lacks provisions for complex cap tables, multi-class shares, and modern financing instruments
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Joint OHADA SAS-I form (shared recommendation with Senegal, DRC). Côte d'Ivoire well-placed to lead the OHADA Council of Ministers initiative.

recommendations.currentText: No clear treatment for stock options and modern talent incentives; mandates formal documentation even for freelance, equity-based, or asynchronous employment models
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Create SYSCOHADA Annex B for labelled startups: specialised accounting treatment for ESOPs, capitalised R&D expenses, and intangible-asset valuation.

recommendations.currentText: Tax exemptions defined but lack clear implementation pathways; multiple agencies with overlapping jurisdiction create administrative duplication
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Auto-apply CGI Article 15 exemption on issuance of Label Startup; single-window registration eliminates submissions to overlapping agencies.

recommendations.currentText: Currency controls restrict foreign transactions; cash-flow challenges for digital banking ventures like Djamo; complex legal adaptations consume 10-15% of investment amounts
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Specialised BCEAO framework for Label-Startup companies: expedited approval, higher transaction limits, simplified documentation for routine intra-group payments and non-resident customer billing.

recommendations.article: Ivory Coast ↔ Senegal (OHADA / UEMOA / ECOWAS pilot)
recommendations.currentText: No mutual recognition with Senegal despite identical regional framework membership and nearly identical qualification criteria
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral passport mirroring the Senegal recommendation: Label Startup recognised by DER/FJ within 14 days, and vice versa. Pilot for OHADA-wide and ECOWAS-wide rollout.

recommendations.code: ECOWAS Supplementary Act on Personal Data Protection (2010)
recommendations.currentText: Duplicative compliance obligations across ECOWAS member states; uneven national implementation of the Supplementary Act
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bring national data-protection rules into full alignment with the ECOWAS Supplementary Act; mutual recognition of data-adequacy determinations across ECOWAS for Label-Startup companies.

labelingBody: National Startup Council (Ministry of Innovation and Technology)
knownGaps: Proclamation does not authorize SAFEs or convertible notes
Implementation regulations still pending across most articles
Forex regime largely unchanged for labeled startups
notable: Tightest age limit (5 years) of any enacted act; broadest reform opportunity since regulations are still being drafted.

recommendations.article: Provisions on PLCs and share companies
recommendations.currentText: Defines PLCs and share companies; multi-class shares and flexible governance are not permitted
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Commercial Code to permit multi-class share structures and single-director governance for Startup-designated companies.

recommendations.currentText: Only recognises debt and equity instruments; SAFEs, convertible notes, and phantom equity are not authorised even for designated startups
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Securities Proclamation to recognise SAFEs, convertible notes, and phantom equity for Startup-designated companies. Treat as equity-linked for accounting, tax, and prudential purposes.

recommendations.code: Ethiopia Labor Code + Income Tax Proclamation
recommendations.currentText: Neither code supports standardised stock-option plans or deferred taxation for equity compensation
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Labor and Income Tax codes to permit ESOPs for Startup-designated companies with taxation deferred to liquidity. Standardised vesting and cliff provisions.

recommendations.code: National Bank of Ethiopia (NBE) Foreign Exchange Directives
recommendations.currentText: NBE approval required for most cross-border transactions; investment flows from foreign VCs routed via offshore entities; governance and financing restrictions force dual structures
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Simplified NBE framework for Startup-designated companies: pre-approved transaction categories, expedited approval (under 5 business days) for routine flows, recognition of standard fintech payment processors.

recommendations.currentText: No mutual startup-label recognition across EAC, COMESA, or AU frameworks; distinct local scorecard for label eligibility
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral pilot with Kenya building on COMESA membership; align Ethiopia Startup-designation criteria with Kenya's Startup Bill scorecard for cross-recognition.

recommendations.currentText: Regulations still pending; broadest reform opportunity precisely because the rules are being written now
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Prioritise implementation regulations enabling: (1) SAFEs/convertibles, (2) multi-class shares, (3) ESOP with tax deferred, (4) auto-applied tax benefits, (5) simplified forex. Sequence drafted with reference to the gaps documented in Tunisia, Senegal, and Nigeria.

knownGaps: Foreign exchange controls remain a binding constraint
No public draft of securities provisions
notable: Already operating an Algerian Startup Fund; act would formalise the existing labelling already in use.

recommendations.currentText: Outbound forex controls bind even routine intra-group transactions; existing Algerian Startup Fund labelling does not include a forex carve-out
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Draft Startup Act should include explicit forex carve-out for labelled startups: pre-approved transaction categories, expedited approval, recognition of fintech payment processors.

recommendations.code: Draft Startup Act + Algerian securities framework
recommendations.currentText: No public draft of securities provisions yet; risk of replicating SAFE-non-recognition gaps from Tunisia, Nigeria, OHADA
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bake SAFE and convertible-note recognition into the Draft Startup Act before enactment. Reference Tunisia's mistakes as the cautionary case.

knownGaps: Significant Economic Presence Tax (SEPT) creates cross-border friction with Uganda/Tanzania
No mutual recognition with EAC partners despite Common Market Protocol Articles 13-14
notable: Largest startup ecosystem in EAC; passing this bill would unlock an EAC-wide passport pilot.

recommendations.currentText: Theoretically enables establishment rights and service provision across EAC borders; not yet implemented in any national startup framework
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Pilot an EAC startup passport leveraging Articles 13-14: KENIA labelling recognised in Rwanda and Uganda within 14 days. Sector-first: fintech then logistics.

recommendations.code: Significant Economic Presence Tax (Finance Act 2022, amended)
recommendations.currentText: Kenyan SEPT creates jurisdictional uncertainty for startups operating cross-border; varying tax obligations across UEMOA and EAC despite regional integration goals
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Harmonise SEPT treatment for EAC-passported startups: single-jurisdiction taxation based on primary place of business; eliminate double taxation for cross-border digital services.

recommendations.currentText: CMA has not classified SAFEs; cautious counsel often re-cast the instrument as a convertible loan with zero coupon
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Startup Bill should explicitly recognise SAFEs and convertible notes; CMA to issue implementing guidance for labelled startups.

recommendations.currentText: Statute permits multi-class shares but practice remains conservative; founders default to one-share-one-vote out of caution
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Codify multi-class share defaults in the Startup Bill for KENIA-labelled startups, with reference articles in the model articles of association.

knownGaps: Securities Industry Act limits convertible instruments
Mandates pension fund 5% allocation to startups by 2026 — outpacing the act itself
notable: Operates the Ghana–Rwanda Fintech Passport (Feb 2025), Africa's first operational cross-border mutual recognition system.

recommendations.currentText: Limits convertible-instrument recognition; pushes founders to issue SAFEs via offshore entities
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend the Securities Industry Act to recognise SAFEs and convertible notes for labelled startups. Ghana's strong fintech-passport posture makes it a natural early adopter.

recommendations.currentText: 5%-by-2026 mandate is in place but lacks the legal vehicle (the Startup Act has not yet enabled labelled startup funds)
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Pass the Startup Act with a licensed-startup-fund category that pension funds can allocate to. Aligns the existing pension mandate with an actual fund vehicle.

recommendations.currentText: Only operational mutual-recognition system in Africa, but limited to fintech and to two countries
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Extend the Ghana–Rwanda model to (1) other sectors (logistics, e-commerce, climate-tech) and (2) other ECOWAS members (Senegal, Nigeria, Ivory Coast). Documented playbook becomes the template for the ECOWAS Startup Regulatory Passport.

knownGaps: Forex restrictions on outbound capital
No SAFE/convertible-note recognition
notable: Strong fintech and B2B SaaS pipeline; potential anchor for a Maghreb passport with Tunisia.

recommendations.currentText: Outbound forex restrictions bind even routine intra-group transactions
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Draft Startup Act should include labelled-startup carve-out: pre-approved transaction categories, expedited approval, recognition of fintech payment processors for non-resident customer billing.

recommendations.code: Draft Startup Act + AMMC (Autorité Marocaine du Marché des Capitaux)
recommendations.currentText: No public draft of securities provisions yet; default frameworks do not recognise SAFEs or convertibles
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bake explicit SAFE/CN recognition into the Draft Startup Act before enactment. AMMC implementing guidance for labelled startups.

recommendations.currentText: No regional recognition framework operational across AMU members despite shared linguistic and regulatory heritage
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral passport with Tunisia once Morocco's framework activates: pilot for AMU-wide rollout including Algeria and Egypt.

knownGaps: Mature regulatory sandbox not yet linked to a national startup act
Cap table and equity-compensation rules under company law remain rigid
notable: Counterpart to the Ghana–Rwanda Fintech Passport; widely seen as Africa's most agile regulator.

recommendations.code: Rwanda Regulatory Sandbox Framework + Draft Startup Law
recommendations.currentText: Sandbox is mature and operational, but not yet linked to startup-label benefits in the Draft Startup Law
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Sandbox graduation auto-confers Startup Label; labelled startups receive expedited sandbox entry. Single-window administered by Rwanda Development Board.

recommendations.currentText: Cap-table and equity-compensation rules remain rigid; default rules push founders to Delaware/Mauritius for governance flexibility
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Company Law to permit multi-class shares and ESOPs with tax deferred to liquidity for labelled startups. Reference articles in the Draft Startup Law.

recommendations.code: Ghana–Rwanda Fintech Passport extension + EAC pilot
recommendations.currentText: Ghana–Rwanda operational only for fintech; EAC Common Market Protocol Articles 13-14 not yet implemented in any national startup framework
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Extend Ghana–Rwanda model to (1) other sectors (logistics, e-commerce, climate-tech) and (2) other EAC members (Kenya, Uganda). Rwanda as the EAC anchor for cross-recognition.

knownGaps: Section 12J expired (2021); no replacement vehicle for venture investing
Exchange control still requires SARB approval for offshore IP
Companies Act allows multi-class shares but practice is conservative
notable: Largest VC market in Africa by absolute deal volume; biggest single-country impact if the bill lands.

recommendations.code: Income Tax Act + replacement for Section 12J
recommendations.currentText: Section 12J expired in 2021 with no replacement; institutional capital lacks a tax-efficient route into early-stage venture
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Legislate a Section-12J successor as part of a Startup Act: tax-credit-backed labelled startup fund vehicle. Anchors the campaign that's currently industry-led.

recommendations.code: South African Reserve Bank (SARB) Exchange Control Regulations
recommendations.currentText: Requires SARB approval for offshore IP transfers; constrains intra-group IP licensing for startups with international structures
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Streamlined notification (not approval) for labelled-startup intra-group IP licensing. Maintains SARB oversight without the multi-month approval delay.

recommendations.currentText: Statute expressly permits multi-class shares; constraint is practice, not statute. Founders default to conservative cap tables out of habit.
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Startup Act campaign should codify multi-class defaults in the model articles for labelled startups, shifting practice rather than statute.

knownGaps: No mechanism to recognise Kenyan startup labelling under the Common Market Protocol
Capital Markets Authority does not register convertible instruments
notable: Could pioneer a Kenya–Uganda EAC passport pilot mirroring the Ghana–Rwanda model.

recommendations.currentText: No mechanism to recognise Kenyan startup labelling despite shared CMP membership
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Bilateral Kenya–Uganda passport built into the Draft Startup Bill: KENIA labelling auto-recognised in Uganda. Pilot for EAC-wide rollout (mirrors Ghana–Rwanda for ECOWAS).

recommendations.currentText: CMA does not register convertible instruments; SAFEs and convertible notes operate in a legal gray area
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Draft Startup Bill should explicitly recognise SAFEs and convertible notes; CMA implementing guidance for labelled startups.

knownGaps: FRA does not recognise SAFEs/convertibles
Forex repatriation rules constrain dollar-denominated revenues
Companies Law caps non-voting shares
notable: Largest North African startup ecosystem by funding; strong fintech and logistics density.

recommendations.currentText: FRA does not recognise SAFEs or convertible notes; pushes founders to issue via Cayman or Delaware parent structures
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Draft Startup Act should explicitly recognise SAFEs and convertible notes; FRA implementing guidance for labelled startups. Treat as equity-linked for prudential reporting.

recommendations.code: Central Bank of Egypt forex regulations
recommendations.currentText: Forex repatriation rules constrain dollar-denominated revenues from non-resident customers; SaaS billing cycles incompatible with current rules
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Labelled-startup carve-out: quarterly rather than daily/30-day reporting; higher transaction limits; recognition of standard fintech payment processors.

recommendations.currentText: Caps non-voting shares; blocks multi-class governance structures used by venture-backed startups
recommendations.proposedAmendment: Amend Companies Law to permit multi-class structures (voting, non-voting, preferred) for labelled startups, with disclosure in the articles of association.
